content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

March 3, 1981


David E. Miller
Law Corporation
Russ Building, Suite 1823
235 Montgomery Street
San Francisco, CA 94104

Re: Opinion Request
No. 81-001

Dear Mr. Miller:

I am in receipt of your request for an opinion pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114 1/ in which you requested an
opinion concerning the reporting obligation of a bona fide
church or religious society under the provisions of the
Political Reform Act. Because your letter raises no substantial
questions involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal
opinion will be issued. I hope, however, the following
response will be helpful.

You asked whether a person promoting a "measure" (as
defined in Section 82043) on behalf of a bona fide church or
religious society would be exempt from the campaign disclosure
provisions if the sole purpose was to protect the public
right to practice the doctrines of said church (Section
86300(c)).

The exemption provided to a person when representing a
bona fide church or religious society solely for the purpose
of protecting the public right to practice the doctrine of
said church under Section 86300(c) applies only to lobbying
disclosure provisions (Chapter 6) of the Political Reform
Act. The exemption in Section 86300(c) does not apply to
the campaign disclosure provisions (Chapter 4) of the Political
Reform Act.


1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise stated.

David E. Miller
March 3, 1981
Page Two


During a telephone conversation on February 19, 1981,
with Rich Eichman of our staff, you asked if the proponent
of a measure is required to disclose a campaign contribution
of $100 or more received from a bona fide church or religious
society. The Political Reform Act does not provide an exemption
for a bona fide church or religious society with respect to
campaign contributions made to candidates or measures.
Furthermore, if a bona fide church or religious society were
to contribute $5,000 or more in a calendar year to candidates
and/or ballot measures, the church or religious society
would be required to file campaign statements as a "Major
Donor Committee."

I hope this response has been helpful. If you wish to
appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any
questions, please contact Rich Eichman at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Executive Director

TP:REiplh

DAVID E. MILLER.
LAW CORPORATION
RUSS BUILDING, SUITE 1823
235 MONTGOMERY STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104

TELEPHONE (415) 362-0254
February 5, 1981

Request No. 81 001

Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Data Received 2-6-81

Response Due 2-20-81

Proposed Response
Due 3-17-81

Honorable Chairman and Commissioners:

This is a request for an opinion of the Commission under Section 83114 of the
California Government Code. I represent a religious institution that is interested in
promoting or opposing an initiative dealing with school vouchers.

The question is whether the promotion of a "measure" (as defined in Section
82043) on behalf of a bona fide church or religious society under the above described
facts would be solely for the purpose of protecting the public right to practice the
doctrines of said church (Section 86300).

I would appreciate your confirming my understanding that Section 86300 exempts
such religious organizations and their agents from the reporting duties otherwise required
of those acting to oppose or support measures.

Thank you for your trouble.

Cordially,

David E. Miller

DEM:vth

sections.conclusion: The exemption provided to a person when representing a bona fide church or religious society solely for the purpose of protecting the public right to practice the doctrine of said church under Section 86300(c) applies only to the lobbying disclosure provisions (Chapter 6) of the Political Reform Act. The exemption in Section 86300(c) does not apply to the campaign disclosure provisions (Chapter 4) of the Political Reform Act. The Political Reform Act does not provide an exemption for a bona fide church or religious society with respect to campaign contributions made to candidates or measures. Furthermore, if a bona fide church or religious society were to contribute $5,000 or more in a calendar year to candidates and/or ballot measures, the church or religious society would be required to file campaign statements as a "Major Donor Committee."
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a religious organization promoting a ballot measure to protect its doctrinal practices is exempt from campaign disclosure provisions under Section 86300(c), and whether campaign contributions from religious organizations must be disclosed.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Section 86300(c) exemption for religious organizations applies only to lobbying disclosure requirements, not campaign disclosure provisions. Religious organizations must disclose campaign contributions and are subject to Major Donor Committee filing requirements if they contribute $5,000 or more annually.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (February 5, 1981) and FPPC response (March 3, 1981). Response letter indicates no formal opinion issued due to lack of substantial interpretive questions, but provides helpful guidance. High confidence in extraction; clear question and conclusion sections present.

CONCLUSION: The exemption provided to a person when representing a bona fide church or religious society solely for the purpose of protecting the public right to practice the doctrine of said church under Section 86300(c) applies only to the lobbying disclosure provisions (Chapter 6) of the Political Reform Act. The exemption in Section 86300(c) does not apply to the campaign disclosure provisions (Chapter 4) of the Political Reform Act. The Political Reform Act does not provide an exemption for a bona fide church or religious society with respect to campaign contributions made to candidates or measures. Furthermore, if a bona fide church or religious society were to contribute $5,000 or more in a calendar year to candidates and/or ballot measures, the church or religious society would be required to file campaign statements as a "Major Donor Committee."

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that while bona fide churches and religious societies are exempt from lobbying disclosure requirements under Section 86300(c), this exemption does not extend to campaign disclosure provisions, and religious organizations must disclose campaign contributions and comply with Major Donor Committee reporting if contributions exceed $5,000 annually.
